title: REVOCAR Act of 2025
plain_summary: This law stops Americans and American companies from investing in or doing business with Venezuela's oil and gas industry. It also bans business with Venezuela's state oil company and Nicolas Maduro's government. The ban will end if Maduro admits he lost the July 2024 election to Edmundo Gonzalez and gives up power, or by December 2027. The President can temporarily allow some business if it is important for American safety, but must tell Congress why.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.
subjects: Presidents and presidential powers, Vice Presidents
Sovereignty, recognition, national governance and status

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Revoke Exemptions for Venezuelan Oil to Curb Autocratic Repression Act of twenty twenty five or the REVOCAR Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. On July twenty eighth, twenty twenty four, more than ten million people in Venezuela voted in a presidential election. Careful records and reports from trusted election watchers clearly showed that the opposition candidate Edmundo Gonzalez received more than two thirds of the votes against the current ruler Nicolas Maduro. The Maduro government has refused to accept that the people of Venezuela chose someone else to lead them. After the election, Maduro's government arrested and mistreated thousands of innocent Venezuelan citizens, including children, just for peacefully taking part in politics.

sections.title: Section 3  Prohibition on Investment by United States Persons in Energy Sector of Venezuela Until the Legitimate Results of the July 28, 2024, Election Are Respected
sections.text: Starting when this law takes effect, certain business deals are banned. No person from the United States or any company owned or controlled by someone from the United States can invest money, trade, or do business in Venezuela's energy sector. This includes providing goods, services, or money to Petroleos de Venezuela (the state oil company) or any of its smaller companies, representatives, or related businesses. It also includes doing business with Nicolas Maduro's government or any other undemocratic government that might replace him in Venezuela. Any business deal that tries to get around this ban or avoid it is also forbidden. These rules apply as allowed by law and government regulations, orders, directions, or special permits that may be issued under this section. The rules apply even if someone signed a contract or got a license or permit before this law was passed. The Secretary of the Treasury, working with the Secretary of State, can take actions including writing regulations to make this section work. The Secretary of the Treasury can use the powers given to the President under sections two hundred three and two hundred five of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to carry out this section. Anyone who breaks these rules, tries to break them, plans with others to break them, or causes someone else to break them will face the same penalties listed in the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. All government agencies must take proper steps within their authority to carry out this section. These bans will end on whichever date comes first. They end when the President tells Congress that Nicolas Maduro has admitted that Edmundo Gonzalez won the July twenty eighth, twenty twenty four election and has given up power to the rightfully elected democratic government in Venezuela. Or they end on December thirty first, twenty twenty seven. The President can cancel these bans for specific situations for up to ninety days at a time. Before doing this, the President must wait at least thirty days and then decide and report to the proper congressional committees that canceling the bans is vital to America's national security interests. The President can extend these cancellations for another ninety days if needed. To do this, the President must make the same decision and submit a report to the proper congressional committees no later than fifteen days before the current cancellation expires. Each report about canceling or extending a cancellation must include specific details about why the cancellation is vital to America's national security interests. It must describe what type of business deal will be allowed and list which banned foreign companies will be involved. It must explain what efforts the United States has made to limit money flows to Nicolas Maduro's government to reduce his ability to harm the Venezuelan people. It must also assess how canceling the bans might affect the chances of achieving a democratic transition in Venezuela, the money available to Maduro or any undemocratic successor government, and the ability of these governments to violate human rights, engage in oppression, or threaten American interests. In this part, the proper congressional committees means the Committee on Foreign Affairs of the House of Representatives, the Committee on Foreign Relations of the Senate, the Committee on Appropriations of the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Appropriations of the Senate. In this section, a United States person means a United States citizen or someone legally allowed to live permanently in the United States. It also means any organization created under United States laws or the laws of any state or territory within the United States, including foreign branches of such organizations. It also means any person physically located in the United States.
